blocks.content: Chapter 18: Capital Budgeting and Valuation with Leverage
WACC – Weighted Average Cost of Capital
The WACC method: A stream of free cash flows discounted by the weighted average cost of capital, rwacc or simply WACC, will obtain the levered value (Enterprise Value) of a company or project.
▪ Common assumptions
▪ Constant debt to equity ratio
▪ Corporate tax is the only imperfection
- Why use WACC?
▪ A company obtain a tax shield by having interest bearing debt
■ The rwacc includes the effect of tax in the valuation
■ If the company is fully financed with equity (zero debt) the rwacc equals re
■ The levered value (VL) is given by the infinite free cash flows discounted by rwacc

blocks.content: Chapter 18: Capital Budgeting and Valuation with Leverage
APV – AdjustedPresent Value
The APV method: A valuation method where we determine the levered value by first calculating the unlevered value and then adding the value of the interest tax shield.
▪ VL VU = APV = + PV (interest tax shield)
▪ The APV method is more complicated than the WACC method because we have to compute both the VU and the present value of the interest tax shield
▪ In addition, implementing the APV approach with a constant debt to equity ratio forces us to solve the project’s debt and value simultaneously

▪ APV is preferable when the debt to equity ratio is not constant. Another positive aspect is that the method also shows the explicit value of the tax shield, which can be interesting information for managers and investors

The FTE method: This is a valuation method where we explicitly calculate the free cash flow available to the equity holders after taking into account all the payments to and from the debt holders.
▪ Instead of determining the free cash flow (FCF) we now determine the free cash flow to equity (FCFE)
▪ The FCFE is the free cash flow that remains after adjusting for interest payments, debt issuance and debt repayments
▪ Remember that we now use the equity cost of capital, re, as the discount rate
▪ There are two major differences from the free cash flow estimates we have used so far:
▪ Interest expenses are deducted before taxes
▪ The net borrowing activity is added as a separate line in the model

blocks.content: Chapter 18: Capital Budgeting and Valuation with Leverage FTE – Flowto equity
▪ The FTE method summarized:
1. Determine the free cash flow to equity of the investment
2. Determine the equity cost of capital, r e
3. Compute the contribution to equity value (E) by discounting the free cash flow to equity (FCFE) by the equity cost of capital (re )
▪ The FTE has the same disadvantage as the APV method as we also here need to compute the project’s debt capacity to determinate the interest and net borrowing
▪ The FTE can offer an advantage if we are trying to compute the total value of the equity of a firm when the capital structure is complex

Chapter 18: Capital Budgeting and Valuation with Leverage
Conclusions
▪ WACC, APV and FTE all give the same results
▪ It is important to understand the WACC, APV and FTE methods to be able to choose the correct method for your specific case
▪ Different leverage policies, financial effects and periodical adjustments, might require alternative implementations of the methods

In real life, a lot of simplifications are made when it comes to valuation of companies. It is however crucial to know how to use the basic methods in this chapter so you understand what implications any form of simplification will have on the value of the company or the project subject to valuation!

Benchmark valuation using comparables
▪ One way to value a company is to compare multiples of similar companies
▪ Investors often use multiples as their main guideline when considering to buy a company
▪ The most common multiples are
■ P/E
- EV/sales
■ EV/EBITDA
- EBITDA/sales
- EV/EBIT
■ Alternative operational multiples: EV/customer, EV/hotel bed
▪ Different multiples are appropriate for different kind of businesses
When conducting a professional valuation, we always compare the multiples of our valuation with comparable companies in the market to see how our estimates fit with similar companies.

blocks.content: Normalresultatmetoden
■ Normalresultat er basert på historisk regnskapsinformasjon, og er en tilnærming til fremtidige kontantstrømmer.
■ Ved bruk av normalresultatmetoden etableres det et normalisert realjustert driftsresultat, som selskapet forventes å kunne levere gitt dagens kapasitet.

■ Deretter diskonteres resultatet etter skatt med et realjustert avkastningskrav for å finne selskapsverdien. Dersom selskapet har lite omsettelige aksjer (for eksempel mindre selskap som ikke er børsnotert), trekkes en illikviditetsrabatt fra for å beregne faktisk selskapsverdi for det spesifikke selskapet som vurderes.

■ Verdien av egenkapitalen estimeres deretter ved å justere selskapsverdien med likvide midler (+), ikke-driftsrelaterte eiendeler (+) og rentebærende/finansiell gjeld (-) og i noen tilfeller unormalt nivå på netto arbeidskapital (+/-)

■ Ved å studere sammenlignbare selskaper/bransjer kan man benytte observerbare nøkkeltall for å estimere verdien av Selskapet.

Deretter estimeres multiplene og en snittbetraktning for peer group (gjennomsnitt eller median) kan benyttes som estimat for objektet som skal vurderes. blocks.content: Chapter 19: Valuation and Financial Modeling
The business plan and financialmodelling
▪ Determining a realistic budget, including sales and operating costs, gives us the opportunity to obtain a more precise valuation
▪ The budget is often provided by the companies themselves, and has to be verified by the analyst
▪ Beware that the company may have incentive for making the budget either expansive or defensive depending on the purpose of the valuation (!)
▪ A valuation is a subjective consideration depending highly on the motive:
▪ Seller: from a seller’s point of view the budgets are often expansive to obtain a highest possible value of the company
▪ Buyer: from a buyer’s point of view the budgets are often defensive to obtain a value as low as possible
▪ Neutral: for tax purposes a neutral budget is used
▪ When entering negotiations you have to take such factors into account

	▪ Buyer: from a buyer’s point of view the budgets are often defensive to obtain a value as low as possible

Valuation - The Normalized Earnings Method
▪ An alternative, and a common valuation method for companies that have shown stable results over a period of time, is the normalized earnings method (normalresultatmetoden)
▪ The goal is to establish a normalized level of the company’s result and then use this normalized level as the terminal for perpetuity
▪ A lot of in debt analysis is required to perform this analysis correctly
▪ With this method we have to adjust the WACC for inflation so it becomes real instead of nominal
▪ In the normalized earnings method, we use the inflation adjusted WACC (real WACC) as the discount factor in the perpetuity formula, with a growth equal to the inflation target of Norges Bank

▪ Look at the price per square meters of similar objects. This is basically the same as the multiples we considered in the Ideko example.

The chart displays the distribution of prices for sold apartments in the Trondheim area over the last year.

There are two histograms presented:

*   **Top histogram:** Shows sold apartments in "Trondheim Øst og Trondheim Sentrum, Leilighet" (Trondheim East and Trondheim Center, Apartment). The total number of sales is 2968. The price range is from approximately 10.000 NOK to 100.000 NOK.
*   **Bottom histogram:** Shows "Trondheim, Alle boliger" (Trondheim, All dwellings). The total number of sales is 5501. The price range is from approximately 10.000 NOK to 100.000 NOK.

The x-axis of both charts represents price in Norwegian Krone (NOK). The y-axis represents the number of sales.
▪ The apartment is 47 square meters
▪ The average price per square meter for this area is 72 100 NOK
▪ By applying this multiple we find that the value of the apartment based on similar prospects would be:
47 sqm x 72 100 NOK/sqm= 3.388.700 NOK

	▪ «To-be»→ Restructuring and changes in the company

<<As-is>> valuations are often related to

	- Mergers and acquisitions

	▪ Change in value between two periods in time

	▪ Measure of efficiency improvement

	▪ Impairment tests

▪ «To-be» valuations are often related to

	▪ Strategic change in the company

	▪ Change in the market situation

	■ Investment decisions

▪ Most of the valuations are a combination of «as-is» and «to-be»

	▪ Status quo in combination with an estimate on present value of growth opportunities

▪ Fremtidig inntjening er alltid usikker

▪ Fastsettelse av avkastningskrav blir ofte tilfeldig

▪ Det er vanskelig å vite hvor lenge inntjeningen kan opprettholdes og hvilke investeringer som må gjøres for å opprettholde inntjeningen

Mandatory Exercise
Valuation of Radionor Communications AS

Perform a valuation of Radionor Communications AS. The valuation should be delivered as a report (max 10 pages) including necessary calculations, financial modeling and explanations of the assumptions made.
Objectives:
1) Make a one-pager company profile, including a short description of the business and the market.
2) Present a short summary the various valuation methods that can be used for this valuation.
3) Find the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) for the company.
• Calculate/estimate the following parameters. Show the calculations and explain your assumptions.
- Beta (B)
Risk free rate (rf ) and market returns (rm )
- Equity cost of capital (re)
- Cost of debt (rd)
4) Estimate an interval for the market capitalization of the company by applying multiples of comparable companies world-wide.

5) Value the company by applying the discounted free cash flow model (DCF).
• Determine a 5 year budget/forecast for the company and establish a financial model based on the forecast
• Find the free cash flows and the terminal value
• Calculate the present value of the free cash flows and estimate the market capitalization
6) Value the company by applying the normalized earnings method (NEM).
• Establish a normalized EBIT for the company (see attachment for procedure)
• Calculate the present value of the free cash flow and estimate the market capitalization
7) Find the implied normalized EBITDA that reflects a share price of NOK 20 billion for the company. Explain your assumptions needed to obtain the normalized EBITDA.
8) Perform a sensitivity analysis with respect to changes in WACC and EBITDA.
9) List the Equity values that you have obtained from your various calculations. Discuss the various values and conclude with your recommendation of what the correct value of the company is.
10) Make a 1 page “Investor teaser” of the company that can be presented to potential investors if the company were to be sold
